summary: Cranial nerve nuclei are organised into seven functional columns based on embryological derivation.
Cranial nerve nuclei are organised into five functional columns.
Cranial nerve nuclei are organised into twelve columns, one for each cranial nerve.
The primary motor cortex is located in the precentral gyrus (Brodmann area 4).
The primary motor cortex is located in the postcentral gyrus.
The primary motor cortex is located in the superior temporal gyrus.
Broca's area in the frontal lobe controls motor speech; damage causes non-fluent (expressive) aphasia.
Broca's area in the temporal lobe controls motor speech.
Broca's area controls language comprehension; damage causes fluent aphasia.
Wernicke's area in the superior temporal gyrus is responsible for language comprehension; damage causes fluent aphasia.
Wernicke's area in the frontal lobe controls language comprehension.
Wernicke's area damage causes non-fluent aphasia with intact comprehension.
The posterior limb of the internal capsule carries corticospinal (motor) fibres; infarction here causes contralateral hemiplegia.
The anterior limb of the internal capsule carries corticospinal fibres.
The genu of the internal capsule carries all corticospinal fibres.
Parkinson's disease involves degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, causing resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia.
Parkinson's disease involves degeneration of cholinergic neurons in the caudate nucleus.
Parkinson's disease is caused by cerebellar degeneration leading to intention tremor.
The motor and sensory homunculi show disproportionately large areas for the face, tongue, and hand.
The homunculus shows equal representation for all body parts.
The lower limb has the largest representation on the lateral surface of the cortex.
